fq_items.question: What are the eligibility requirements for the PMP exam?
fq_items.answer: PMI requires a four-year degree with 36 months of project leadership experience, or a high school diploma with 60 months of experience. All applicants also need 35 hours of PM education, which our PMP Certification Training satisfies.

fq_items.question: How long does it take to get PMP certified?
fq_items.answer: After completing your training, PMI typically approves your application within 5–10 business days. Once approved, you have one year to schedule and pass the exam. Most candidates take 4–8 weeks to study after their course.

fq_items.question: What is the CAPM and who should pursue it?
fq_items.answer: The Certified Associate in Project Management is designed for professionals early in their PM career or transitioning into project management. It requires only secondary education and 23 hours of training — no professional experience is required.

fq_items.question: What is CPMAI and how does it differ from PMP?
fq_items.answer: The CPMAI credential, offered by Cognilytica, focuses on managing projects that involve artificial intelligence. It covers the AI project lifecycle, data strategy, and governance. It complements rather than replaces the PMP.

fq_items.question: Do your courses satisfy PMI’s education hour requirement?
fq_items.answer: Yes. Our PMP training provides the 35 contact hours required for the PMP exam application. Our CAPM training provides the 23 hours required for the CAPM application. You receive a certificate of completion to submit to PMI.

fq_items.question: Are the courses available entirely online?
fq_items.answer: Yes. All of our programs are offered as live-virtual cohorts conducted via video conference — no travel required. We also offer self-paced on-demand access for PMP and CAPM courses. Both formats include the same study materials and instructor support.

1.title: PMP vs. CAPM: Which PMI Certification Should You Pursue First?
1.author: Granite PM Academy
1.excerpt: Both are legitimate PMI credentials — but they're built for different career stages. Here's how to decide which one to pursue first.

content_blocks.text: If you're early in a project management career, the choice between the PMP and the CAPM can feel confusing — both are PMI credentials, both appear on job descriptions, and neither is obviously wrong. The difference comes down to where you are right now, not where you want to end up.

The PMP requires either a four-year degree with 36 months of project leadership experience, or a high school diploma with 60 months. If you don't have that experience logged, you cannot apply — PMI audits applications and will reject those that don't meet the threshold.The CAPM requires only secondary education (a high school diploma) and 23 contact hours of project management training. No professional experience required. This makes it genuinely accessible to students, career changers, and coordinators who are managing work but haven't been titled as project managers yet.

The PMP is harder — there's no way around it. It covers predictive, agile, and hybrid project management across 180 questions, and PMI intentionally writes situational questions that test judgment rather than recall. You need to understand not just what the PMBOK says but how an experienced PM would actually respond in a given situation.The CAPM is 150 questions focused primarily on PMBOK concepts and process groups. It rewards solid study of the reference material. Many candidates who took both describe the CAPM as a strong foundation — it forces you to internalize framework vocabulary that the PMP later tests in context.

The PMP has broader name recognition and appears more frequently in mid-to-senior job postings. Salary surveys consistently show PMP holders earning more than their non-certified counterparts — PMI's own data puts the median salary premium at around 16% in the US.The CAPM matters most in industries where PMI credentials are valued but the PMP is out of reach due to experience requirements: government contractors, IT project coordination, construction project support, and entry-level roles at consulting firms. In those contexts, a CAPM distinguishes you from candidates with no certification at all.The CAPM is also a stepping stone. Several graduates earn it early, gain the required experience while employed, and then pursue the PMP with a much stronger foundation than candidates who start fresh.

Less than 36 months of PM leadership experience? Start with the CAPM. You'll build knowledge and credibility while accumulating the hours you need for the PMP.36+ months of experience and a four-year degree? Go directly to the PMP. The CAPM won't add much if you already qualify for the more recognized credential.Experienced PM who never got certified? Skip the CAPM entirely. Pursue the PMP.Currently in a support or coordinator role wanting to move into PM? The CAPM signals intent and knowledge to hiring managers before your title catches up.

content_blocks.cb_subtext: Both courses are available live-virtual and on-demand. Our team can help you pick the right starting point.

2.title: Scrum vs. Kanban vs. SAFe: What the PMI-ACP Exam Actually Wants You to Know
2.author: Granite PM Academy
2.excerpt: The PMI-ACP covers Scrum, Kanban, XP, and SAFe — but it doesn't test them equally. Here's where the exam actually puts its weight.

content_blocks.text: Most PMI-ACP candidates know the exam covers agile — what trips them up is expecting it to work like a Scrum Master certification. The PMI-ACP is framework-agnostic by design. It doesn't test whether you can recite Scrum ceremonies or Kanban WIP limits. It tests whether you can apply agile thinking across situations that may not fit neatly into any single framework.

PMI structures the PMI-ACP exam around seven domains rather than specific frameworks:Agile Principles and MindsetValue-Driven DeliveryStakeholder EngagementTeam PerformanceAdaptive PlanningProblem Detection and ResolutionContinuous ImprovementNotice what's missing: there's no 'Scrum Domain' or 'SAFe Domain.' PMI draws from a broad reading list — Scrum, Kanban, XP, DSDM, Crystal, and Lean — but the questions ask you to apply principles, not recite ceremonies.
content_blocks.heading_text: How the PMI-ACP Exam Content Is Organized

content_blocks.text: Scrum concepts — sprints, retrospectives, backlog refinement, velocity, burndown charts — appear frequently because Scrum is the most widely practiced agile framework and most candidates have direct experience with it. But PMI won't ask you to recite the Sprint Review definition. It will ask what you should do when a retrospective isn't producing useful improvements, or how you handle a stakeholder who keeps adding scope mid-sprint. Those questions require judgment, not memorization.

Kanban principles — visualizing work, limiting WIP, managing flow — appear most heavily in the Value-Driven Delivery and Continuous Improvement domains. Lean shows up in waste identification and process optimization questions. These aren't deep dives; they're applied: 'Your team is struggling with context switching. What approach would help most?' If you know that Kanban's core mechanism is WIP limits and why they reduce multitasking overhead, you'll answer correctly.
content_blocks.heading_text: Where Kanban and Lean Show Up

content_blocks.text: SAFe appears in the exam reading list but carries less weight than Scrum or Kanban. You're more likely to encounter scaled agile concepts in questions about coordinating multiple teams, managing dependencies, and aligning program increments to strategy — rather than questions about specific SAFe roles or ceremonies. If you work in a SAFe environment, your practical experience will help. If not, don't invest heavy study time here at the expense of core agile principles.

Study the underlying principles first — the Agile Manifesto, Lean thinking, servant leadership — before framework specifics. A candidate who deeply understands why agile works the way it does will outperform one who memorized Scrum ceremonies but can't reason through novel situations. Then layer in framework specifics (especially Scrum) and practice with scenario-based questions rather than definition flashcards.

3.title: Why the PMI-RMP Is the Most Underrated PMI Certification (and What It Pays)
3.author: Granite PM Academy
3.excerpt: The PMI-RMP rarely shows up on 'top certifications' lists. That's exactly why it's worth getting — and what it typically pays.

content_blocks.text: Ask a room full of project managers which PMI credentials they're aware of and you'll hear PMP every time, CAPM and PMI-ACP from most of them, and maybe PgMP from a few. The PMI-RMP rarely comes up — and that's a meaningful gap in how the profession thinks about specialization.

The PMI Risk Management Professional credential validates your ability to assess and identify project risks, mitigate threats, and capitalize on opportunities. It's not a general PM credential — it's a specialist credential that signals you understand how to build and execute a risk management strategy, not just check the risk register box during planning.PMI designed it for experienced PMs who focus heavily on risk, program managers responsible for risk across multiple projects, and practitioners in industries where risk identification failures have catastrophic consequences: defense, construction, healthcare IT, financial services, and infrastructure.

To apply for the PMI-RMP you need:A secondary degree (high school diploma) with 4,500 hours of project risk management experience and 40 contact hours of PM education focused on risk, ORA four-year degree with 3,000 hours of project risk management experience and 30 contact hours of PM education focused on riskNote that this requires risk-specific experience hours, not general PM hours. If you've been managing risk registers, facilitating risk workshops, and running qualitative and quantitative analysis in your projects, you likely qualify without realizing it.

PMI's Earning Power salary survey shows PMI-RMP holders earn a meaningful premium over non-certified counterparts in risk-intensive industries. In the US, PMI-RMP holders in defense and government contracting, large-scale construction, and financial services consistently appear in the top quartile of PM compensation ranges. The credential is comparatively rare — there are roughly 5,000 active PMI-RMP holders globally versus over 1 million PMP holders — which means it stands out immediately on a resume in industries that value it.

The PMI-RMP makes the most sense if you already hold a PMP and want to differentiate in a risk-heavy industry or role. It's a strong complement to the PMP rather than a substitute — hiring managers in fields like aerospace, defense contracting, or infrastructure rarely see a PMP + PMI-RMP combination, which is exactly why it carries weight when they do.It's less compelling if your work doesn't involve meaningful risk identification and management, or if you're in an industry where risk is treated as a compliance checkbox rather than a project management discipline.

4.title: How to Make Your Employer Pay for Your PMI Certification (Email Template Included)
4.author: Granite PM Academy
4.excerpt: Most employers will pay for PMI certification training — if you ask the right way. Here's the framework and an email template you can send today.
4.featured_image_alt: Getting employer to pay for PMP certification

content_blocks.text: A surprising number of project managers pay for PMI certification out of pocket when their employer would have covered it — sometimes fully — if they'd asked. Most companies with any kind of training budget treat professional certification as a legitimate business expense. The barrier is usually not the money; it's that the employee never made the case.

Before drafting anything, check your employee handbook for a tuition reimbursement or professional development policy. Many mid-to-large employers have a formal program — often $2,000–$5,000 per year — that managers don't proactively advertise. HR can confirm what's available and what the approval process looks like. If a formal policy exists, follow it exactly. This turns your ask into an administrative process rather than a negotiation.

If there's no formal policy, or if the amount you need exceeds the standard limit, you need to make the case in terms your manager and finance team recognize:Direct ROI: PMP-certified PMs run projects with fewer scope changes, cost overruns, and schedule slips — the PMI Pulse of the Profession data backs this up. Quantify what that means for your organization's current project portfolio if you can.Retention: It's cheaper to sponsor certification than to replace a credentialed PM. The cost of recruiting and onboarding a replacement is typically 50–150% of annual salary.Client requirements: Some government contracts and enterprise clients explicitly require or prefer PMP-certified PMs on their accounts. If this applies to your role, it's your strongest argument.Competitive positioning: If competitors' delivery teams hold PMPs and yours don't, that gap affects client confidence.

Specificity closes deals. Don't ask for 'help with my PMP.' Come with:The exact course you want, provider, and costThe PMI exam fee ($405 for members, $555 for non-members)The PMI membership fee if applicable ($139/year)The total request — usually $2,500–$3,000 all inThe training dates and how they affect your work schedule (ideally minimal impact)

Here's a version you can adapt:Subject: Professional Development Request — PMP CertificationHi [Manager's name],I'd like to request company sponsorship for my PMP certification. I've identified a training provider and have mapped out the path to sitting for the exam.Here's what I'm requesting:PMP Certification Training (4-day live-virtual): $[cost]PMI exam fee: $405 (member) or $555 (non-member)PMI annual membership (optional but reduces exam cost): $139Total: approximately $[total]The training runs [dates] and is fully online, so there's no travel or significant time away from projects beyond the training days themselves.The PMP has a direct impact on delivery quality — PMI research shows certified PMs complete projects within budget at a higher rate, and several of our [client/contract type] accounts either require or strongly prefer PMP-certified leads. I'm happy to discuss further if useful.Thanks for considering it.[Your name]

Ask about a split arrangement — they fund the training, you fund the exam fee, or vice versa. Ask whether there's a fiscal year timing issue (some training budgets reset in January or April) and request that they approve it for next quarter. If they decline entirely, the PMP is still worth pursuing on your own — the salary premium typically recovers the cost within months.

5.title: PMI-PBA vs. CBAP: Which Business Analysis Certification Is Right for Project Managers?
5.author: Granite PM Academy
5.excerpt: Both the PMI-PBA and CBAP target business analysis — but they're built for different audiences. Here's how to choose between them.

content_blocks.text: If you're a project manager who also handles business analysis work — requirements gathering, stakeholder elicitation, solution evaluation — you've probably come across both the PMI-PBA and the CBAP. Both are respected credentials. Both are harder to earn than they look. The difference is in who they're designed for and what they signal to employers.

PMI-PBA (PMI Professional in Business Analysis) is offered by the Project Management Institute and sits within the PMI credential family alongside the PMP, PMI-ACP, and PMI-RMP. It focuses on business analysis within the context of projects — requirements management, stakeholder engagement, solution evaluation, and traceability across the project lifecycle.CBAP (Certified Business Analysis Professional) is offered by IIBA (International Institute of Business Analysis) and is the IIBA's senior-level credential. It covers the full BABOK (Business Analysis Body of Knowledge) and is designed for practitioners whose primary role is business analysis, not project management.

RequirementPMI-PBACBAPExperience4,500 hours BA experience (degree) or 7,500 hours (no degree)7,500 hours BA experience in past 10 yearsEducation contact hours35 hours of BA education21 hours of BA professional development in past 4 yearsReferencesNot required2 references from career managers or CBAP holders

If you're a PM who regularly does BA work but your primary identity is project management, the PMI-PBA is the better fit. It positions you within the PMI ecosystem — organizations that already value the PMP will recognize the PMI-PBA as a natural extension. It signals that you can manage both delivery and requirements without needing a dedicated BA on every project.If your role has shifted to being primarily a business analyst — you're leading requirements efforts, working independently of a PM, and operating across multiple projects rather than leading one — the CBAP carries more weight with IIBA-oriented employers and clients who view BA as a distinct discipline.
content_blocks.heading_text: Which One Should Project Managers Pursue?

content_blocks.text: The PMI-PBA tends to resonate more in project-driven environments: consulting, IT delivery, construction, government contracting. The CBAP tends to carry more weight in organizations with mature BA practices: financial services, insurance, large enterprise IT, and healthcare. Both are globally recognized — the geographic and industry context matters more than a universal ranking.

6.title: The 4-Week PMP Study Plan That Works for Full-Time Working PMs
6.author: Granite PM Academy
6.excerpt: A realistic four-week PMP study plan for people who still have a job, a schedule, and a life — with daily time allocations and weekly milestones.
6.featured_image_alt: PMP study plan for working professionals

content_blocks.text: Most PMP study guides are written for people with unlimited time. This one isn't. Four weeks is achievable if you treat it like a real project — defined scope, allocated time, clear milestones, and a plan for when things go off track.This plan assumes you've completed a PMI-authorized training course (which gives you the 35 contact hours you need to apply). If you haven't, do that first — the course gives you the foundation this plan builds on.

Plan for 90 minutes per weekday and 3 hours on Saturday. Sunday is a buffer — use it if you fall behind; protect it if you don't. That's roughly 9.5 hours per week, about 38–40 hours total. Research on PMP preparation suggests 35–50 hours of focused study after completing training is where most candidates are ready to test. This plan hits the floor.If 90 minutes per weekday isn't realistic, extend to five weeks at 60 minutes per day. Don't compress below 60 minutes — you lose the cognitive load needed to develop exam-level judgment.

Goal: Solid command of the predictive (waterfall) domains — scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk, procurement.Daily routine: Read one domain area. Do 15–20 practice questions on that area. Review every wrong answer and understand why the correct answer is right — not just that it is.Saturday: Do a 60-question timed practice exam (predictive only). Aim for 70%+ before moving on.Key mindset: The PMP doesn't ask what the PMBOK says — it asks what an experienced PM would do. When two answers both seem correct, pick the one that addresses root cause and involves stakeholders appropriately.

Goal: Comfortable with agile principles, Scrum mechanics, and hybrid situations. Approximately half the PMP exam is agile or hybrid — don't underweight this.Daily routine: One agile topic per day: Agile Manifesto + principles, Scrum roles and events, Kanban + flow, hybrid planning, servant leadership. 15–20 practice questions per day.Saturday: 60-question mixed predictive/agile practice exam. Target 72%+.Common trap: Agile questions often involve team dynamics, conflict, and stakeholder communication. The right answer usually involves transparency, early feedback, and empowering the team — not escalating or imposing process.

Goal: Project integration, change management, benefits realization, and business environment. These appear throughout the exam rather than in isolated clusters.Daily routine: Integration and change control (Day 1–2), stakeholder engagement and communication (Day 3), organizational change and benefits (Day 4), ethics and professional responsibility (Day 5).Saturday: Full 120-question timed practice exam. Target 74%+. Review every question you weren't fully confident on — not just wrong answers.

Goal: Test endurance and close specific gaps.Day 1–2: Review your week 3 practice exam. Identify your two lowest-scoring areas. Study only those.Day 3–4: Two more 60-question timed sets, one per day, focused on your weak areas.Day 5: Full 180-question simulation under real test conditions — timed, no breaks beyond what PMI allows. If you score 75%+, you're ready. If not, extend one more week on weak areas.Day before the exam: Light review only. No new material. Sleep.

The PMP exam has 180 questions and 230 minutes. That's about 77 seconds per question. Practice under time pressure in Week 4 — candidates who haven't simulated the time constraint often find it more disorienting than the content itself.

7.title: The 7 PMP Exam Mistakes That Kill First-Time Pass Rates — and How to Avoid Every One
7.author: Granite PM Academy
7.excerpt: These seven mistakes show up in almost every PMP exam failure. Knowing them in advance is the difference between first-attempt success and a retake.
7.featured_image_alt: PMP exam preparation and mistakes to avoid

content_blocks.text: The PMP exam doesn't fail people randomly. After working with thousands of candidates, the patterns are clear — the same mistakes show up across almost every first-attempt failure. Most of them aren't knowledge gaps. They're strategic errors that cause candidates who know the material to pick the wrong answer anyway.

The PMP exam doesn't test whether you can recall PMBOK definitions. It tests whether you can apply PM judgment in realistic situations. Candidates who spend most of their prep time memorizing inputs, tools, and outputs find that the exam barely tests those — and they've neglected the situational reasoning that roughly 70% of questions require. Read the PMBOK for context. Do practice questions for competency.
content_blocks.heading_text: Mistake 1: Studying the PMBOK Like a Textbook

content_blocks.text: Approximately half of the PMP exam is agile or hybrid. Many candidates with traditional PM backgrounds study agile as an afterthought — a few days at the end of their prep. That's not enough. Agile questions require you to think differently about authority, planning, and stakeholder communication. Budget equal time for agile and predictive content.

PMI writes questions from the perspective of an ideal project manager operating in a well-structured organization. In real life, you might escalate to the sponsor, work around a difficult stakeholder, or accept risk because the fix costs too much. On the exam, the ideal PM communicates proactively, resolves issues at the lowest possible level, follows the change control process, and always documents. When your gut says 'in real life I would never do that,' the exam answer is probably still the textbook one.
content_blocks.heading_text: Mistake 3: Picking the 'Feels Right' Answer Instead of the PM Answer

content_blocks.text: 180 questions in 230 minutes is 77 seconds per question. That's enough time if you're not overthinking — but candidates who've never practiced timed often find the clock is the hardest part of the exam. Start doing full timed simulations at least two weeks before your test date. You need to build the pace reflex, not just content knowledge.
content_blocks.heading_text: Mistake 4: Not Practicing Under Time Pressure

content_blocks.text: When you score a practice exam and get 78%, most candidates look at what they got wrong. The better habit is to review every question you weren't completely certain about — including the ones you got right by guessing. If you can't explain in one sentence why the correct answer is right and the other three are wrong, you don't actually know that question. You got lucky. Luck doesn't compound; reasoning does.

PMI gives you one year after approval to sit for the exam. Many candidates use most of it — studying leisurely, waiting until they feel 'fully ready.' The problem is that without a concrete exam date, study intensity stays low. Book your exam date before you start studying. Four to eight weeks after finishing your training course is the right window. A real deadline changes how you use every hour.
content_blocks.heading_text: Mistake 6: Scheduling the Exam Too Late

content_blocks.text: PMI includes ethics and professional responsibility questions on every exam. They're not hard — but candidates who've never explicitly reviewed PMI's Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct get tripped up. These questions typically involve situations where you discover a colleague did something questionable, or where you're asked to do something you're uncomfortable with. PMI's answers prioritize transparency, integrity, and professional disclosure. Read the Code once, understand the pattern, and these become easy points.

content_blocks.hs_caption2: 23 Contact Hours Included · The Stepping Stone to PMP

content_blocks.text:
The CAPM (Certified Associate in Project Management) is PMI's entry-level project management credential — the industry's recognized starting point for professionals entering or transitioning into project management roles.

Unlike the PMP, the CAPM has no professional PM experience requirement. You need 23 hours of formal PM education and a secondary school diploma. That's it. Our 3-day CAPM certification training satisfies those 23 hours and fully prepares you for the exam.

The CAPM covers the full scope of project management — initiation, planning, execution, monitoring, and closing — across both predictive (waterfall) and agile methodologies. PMI updated the CAPM exam in 2023 to reflect the growing importance of agile and hybrid project delivery, so our curriculum is fully aligned to the current exam content outline.

Our 3-day course is available as live-virtual instructor-led training ($795) or self-paced on-demand ($495), both formats satisfying PMI's 23-hour requirement.

The CAPM is also widely used as a structured pathway to the PMP. Once you've accumulated the required PM experience, your CAPM training hours count toward the PMP's 35-hour requirement — no need to repeat foundational coursework.

content_blocks.wb_heading: The CAPM — Your Entry Point Into PMI's Credentialing System
content_blocks.wb_subtext: The CAPM is recognized globally by employers who hire for PM roles. It proves you understand PMI's framework, methodology, and vocabulary — before you have years of experience to prove it another way. And because your 23 training hours apply toward the PMP's 35-hour requirement, the CAPM isn't just a credential — it's the first step in a defined certification pathway.

fq_items.question: What are the CAPM eligibility requirements?
fq_items.answer: PMI requires: (1) a secondary school diploma (high school diploma or equivalent); and (2) 23 hours of project management education. There is no professional PM experience requirement. Our 3-day CAPM training satisfies the 23-hour requirement.

fq_items.question: What is the difference between CAPM and PMP?
fq_items.answer: The PMP requires 36 months of documented PM experience; the CAPM has no experience requirement. The CAPM is the entry-level credential for professionals entering PM, while the PMP is the senior professional credential. CAPM training hours count toward the PMP's 35-hour requirement, making the CAPM a structured pathway to the PMP.

fq_items.question: Is the CAPM worth it in 2026?
fq_items.answer: Yes, particularly for professionals who don't yet have the experience for the PMP. The CAPM is recognized by employers globally as evidence of formal PM knowledge and PMI alignment. It frequently differentiates candidates in hiring — especially for coordinator, analyst, and associate PM roles.

fq_items.question: How long does the CAPM exam take and what format is it?
fq_items.answer: The CAPM exam is 150 multiple-choice questions with a 3-hour time limit. It's administered by Pearson VUE at test centers or online proctored. Our training includes timed mock exams so you're fully familiar with the format before test day.

fq_items.question: What is included in the CAPM certification training?
fq_items.answer: 23 contact hours of instruction satisfying PMI's education requirement; 2023+ Exam Content Outline-aligned curriculum with agile and predictive coverage; targeted practice questions; full-length mock exam; PMI application walkthrough; and 30-day post-course study plan. The CAPM exam fee ($225 for PMI members, $300 non-members) is paid separately to PMI.

fq_items.question: How long does it take to get CAPM certified?
fq_items.answer: After completing our 3-day training, most students submit their PMI application within a week. PMI reviews applications in 5–10 business days. After approval, you schedule with Pearson VUE and sit the exam within 1–3 weeks. Most students are CAPM-certified within 4–6 weeks of completing training.

fq_items.question: Do my CAPM training hours apply toward the PMP?
fq_items.answer: Yes. The 23 contact hours from your CAPM training count directly toward the PMP's 35-hour education requirement. When you're ready to pursue the PMP, you only need to document additional hours to reach 35 total — or in many cases, our CAPM course can be extended to the full 35 hours.

content_blocks.hs_subtext: AI is reshaping how projects are delivered. The CPMAI (Certified Professional in AI for Project Management) gives you the credential to lead AI-driven initiatives with confidence — covering AI project lifecycle, responsible AI governance, machine learning fundamentals for PMs, and practical AI tool application. 3-Day Live-Virtual or On-Demand · PMI Premier Authorized · AI + PM Expertise · Practical, Not Theoretical

content_blocks.text:
The CPMAI (Certified Professional in AI for Project Management) is a specialized credential for project managers who are leading, managing, or contributing to AI-driven projects. It's designed to bridge the gap between project management expertise and the unique challenges AI projects present — without requiring a data science background.

AI projects fail at significantly higher rates than traditional IT projects. The primary cause isn't technical — it's poor project management. AI initiatives have unique lifecycle characteristics, data dependencies, iterative model development cycles, governance requirements, and organizational change implications that traditional PM frameworks don't fully address.

The CPMAI gives project managers the vocabulary, frameworks, and practical skills to manage AI projects effectively — from scoping and data governance to responsible AI, model deployment, and measuring business value.

Our 3-day CPMAI training is available as live-virtual instructor-led training ($1,495) or self-paced on-demand ($795). Both formats are available through Granite PM Academy as a PMI Premier Authorized Training Partner.

The CPMAI is suitable for PMP-holders and experienced PMs who want to specialize in AI project delivery, as well as technology leaders and business analysts who are managing or contributing to AI initiatives without formal PM credentials.

content_blocks.wb_heading: The CPMAI — Your Credential for the AI Era of Project Management
content_blocks.wb_subtext: AI is not a future concern for project managers — it's a present one. Organizations are deploying AI across operations, products, and services at accelerating speed. The PMs who understand how to scope, govern, and deliver AI projects will lead the work that matters most over the next decade. The CPMAI is the credential that marks that expertise.

fq_items.question: What is the CPMAI certification?
fq_items.answer: The CPMAI (Certified Professional in AI for Project Management) is a specialized credential for project managers who lead, manage, or contribute to AI-driven projects. It covers the AI project lifecycle, data management for PMs, responsible AI governance, regulatory compliance, risk management, and practical AI tool application — without requiring a data science background.

fq_items.question: Do I need a data science or technical background for the CPMAI?
fq_items.answer: No. The CPMAI is designed for project managers, not data scientists. The curriculum addresses AI at the level a PM needs to lead AI projects effectively — understanding concepts, managing data-dependent work, governing AI risks, and measuring business value. You do not need coding, statistics, or ML engineering skills.

fq_items.question: Who should get the CPMAI?
fq_items.answer: PMP-holders and experienced PMs who want to specialize in AI project delivery; technology and IT program managers overseeing digital transformation; business analysts working on AI initiatives; product managers building AI-powered products; and PMO leaders who need to govern AI projects consistently across a portfolio.

fq_items.question: Is the CPMAI a PMI credential?
fq_items.answer: The CPMAI is delivered through Granite PM Academy as a PMI Premier Authorized Training Partner. We are authorized by PMI to deliver this credential training. For the most current information on PMI's recognition of specific AI credentials, we recommend verifying directly at pmi.org.

fq_items.question: What is included in the CPMAI training?
fq_items.answer: 3 days of instruction (live-virtual or on-demand); AI project lifecycle and framework content; responsible AI and governance modules; data management for PMs; regulatory compliance (GDPR, EU AI Act overview); AI risk management; practical AI tool guidance; and CPMAI exam preparation. The certification exam fee is paid separately to the certifying body.

fq_items.question: What is the difference between live-virtual and on-demand CPMAI training?
fq_items.answer: Live-virtual is a 3-day instructor-led course in a real-time online classroom — a subject-matter expert guides every session and answers questions live. On-demand gives you the same curriculum as self-paced recorded content you can access immediately on your own schedule. Both formats deliver the full CPMAI curriculum.

fq_items.question: Why is AI project management a distinct skill from regular project management?
fq_items.answer: AI projects have fundamentally different characteristics from traditional IT projects: data is both a dependency and a deliverable; model performance is probabilistic, not binary; deployment introduces ongoing monitoring requirements; and regulatory and ethical governance add dimensions that traditional PM frameworks weren't designed to handle. AI projects fail at higher rates than traditional IT projects, and the primary cause is PM-level, not technical.

fq_items.question: Can my employer pay for CPMAI training?
fq_items.answer: Yes. CPMAI training qualifies as professional development in most organizational L&D budgets. We provide formal invoicing and purchase orders. For organizations standing up AI programs, group pricing and dedicated sessions are available — contact us for a custom quote.

content_blocks.hs_heading: Earn PDUs to Maintain Your PMI Certification
content_blocks.hs_subtext: PMI requires credential holders to earn Professional Development Units (PDUs) every three years to maintain active status. Granite PM Academy offers PDU-eligible courses across all PMI certification domains.

fc_items.heading: PMP® — 60 PDUs
fc_items.text: PMP holders must earn 60 PDUs every three years across Education and Giving Back categories. At least 35 PDUs must be in Education.

fc_items.heading: PMI-ACP® — 30 PDUs
fc_items.text: PMI-ACP holders must earn 30 PDUs every three years. At least 18 must be in agile-related Education activities.

fc_items.heading: PMI-RMP® — 30 PDUs
fc_items.text: PMI-RMP holders must earn 30 PDUs every three years. At least 18 must be in risk management-related Education topics.

content_blocks.text: PDUs are divided into two main categories under the PMI Talent Triangle:Education PDUs — earned through courses, training, webinars, and self-directed learning. These must make up the majority of your renewal PDUs.Giving Back PDUs — earned by creating content, volunteering, or working as a practitioner in project management.Courses completed through Granite PM Academy qualify for Education PDUs. You report PDUs directly through your PMI dashboard at pmi.org.

fq_items.question: Can I use a Granite PM Academy course for PDUs?
fq_items.answer: Yes. As a PMI Premier Authorized Training Partner, our courses are pre-approved for PDUs. You'll receive a certificate of completion to submit when reporting PDUs through your PMI dashboard.

fq_items.question: When do I need to report my PDUs?
fq_items.answer: PMI requires PDU reporting within your three-year certification cycle. You can track your cycle start date and progress in your PMI myPMI account at pmi.org.

fq_items.question: How do I report PDUs to PMI?
fq_items.answer: Log into pmi.org, go to your certification dashboard, and click 'Report PDUs.' Select 'Education' as the category, enter the course name, dates, and number of hours.

fq_items.question: Do I need to take the full course or can I take part of it?
fq_items.answer: You earn PDUs for the hours you complete. If a course is 8 hours, you earn 8 PDUs. PMI tracks by contact hours, not by course completion percentage.

content_blocks.text:
Granite PM Academy was founded by practitioners who spent 30+ years inside the work — not in classrooms teaching it from the outside. Our founders came up through large consulting firms, managing complex, high-stakes programs for enterprise clients before moving into executive leadership. That path included serving as CIO of a $1B+ organization, where the gap between credentialed project managers and genuinely capable ones became impossible to ignore.

What we saw, repeatedly, was PMs who had passed their exams but had never been taught how to think through a real project decision. They had frameworks memorized but no instinct for applying them. The certifications existed. The fundamentals weren't sticking.

That's the problem Granite PM Academy was built to solve. We teach PM the way it actually works — grounded in the methodology PMI tests, but delivered by people who have run real projects, managed real teams, and made real decisions under pressure. Every course, every instructor, and every curriculum update starts from the same question: does this make someone a better project manager, or just a better test-taker?

We believe those outcomes should be the same thing. That's why our pass rates are what they are — and why our graduates come back when they're ready for the next credential.

Project management certifications open doors — but only if candidates are genuinely prepared. Granite PM Academy exists to close the gap between what training programs teach and what the PMI exams actually test.

Every curriculum update, every instructor hire, and every course format decision is made with one goal: helping project managers pass their exams on the first attempt and carry real skills back to their organizations.

content_blocks.hs_subtext: Answers to the most common questions about PMP, CAPM, and CPMAI certification training — eligibility requirements, exam format, scheduling, pricing, the pass guarantee, and what happens after you enroll.

fq_items.question: What are the eligibility requirements for the PMP?
fq_items.answer: PMI requires: (1) a four-year degree plus 36 months of documented project management experience, OR a high school diploma plus 60 months of PM experience; and (2) 35 hours of formal PM education. Our PMP certification training and PMP bootcamp both satisfy the 35-hour requirement.

fq_items.question: How long does it take to get PMP certified?
fq_items.answer: With our live-virtual training, most students submit their PMI application on the final day of class. PMI approves applications in 5–10 business days. After approval, students schedule with Pearson VUE and typically sit the exam within 30–45 days of completing training.

fq_items.question: What is the difference between PMP Certification Training and the PMP Bootcamp?
fq_items.answer: Both are 4-day live-virtual courses that satisfy PMI's 35-hour requirement. The PMP Certification Training ($1,995) follows a standard daily schedule (9am–5pm). The PMP Bootcamp ($2,495) is a more intensive format (9am–6pm) with additional exam simulation time and is structured specifically for students who want the fastest possible path to exam readiness. Both are covered by our Pass Guarantee.

fq_items.question: What is your Pass Guarantee?
fq_items.answer: If you complete our course, follow our post-course study plan, and don't pass the PMP exam on your first attempt, we enroll you in the next available session at no cost. No fine print beyond those two conditions.

fq_items.question: How many questions are on the PMP exam?
fq_items.answer: The PMP exam has 180 questions and a 4-hour time limit. Approximately half the questions are scenario-based, testing how you apply PMI's framework rather than testing memorization. Our training includes full 180-question timed mock exams.

fq_items.question: What is the PMP exam fee?
fq_items.answer: PMI charges $405 for members and $555 for non-members. PMI membership costs $139/year and includes a free digital copy of the PMBOK Guide, making membership the better financial choice for most candidates. The exam fee is paid directly to PMI and is separate from our training cost.

fq_items.question: Is the PMP exam available online?
fq_items.answer: Yes. The PMP is available as an online proctored exam through Pearson VUE — you can take it from home or at a Pearson VUE test center. Both options are identical in content and scoring.

fq_items.question: How current is the PMP exam content?
fq_items.answer: PMI updates the PMP Exam Content Outline periodically. The current version (effective 2021, with ongoing updates) requires approximately 50% agile and hybrid content — a significant shift from earlier versions. Our curriculum is fully updated to reflect the current outline, including the 2026 weighting.

fq_items.question: What are the CAPM eligibility requirements?
fq_items.answer: PMI requires a secondary school diploma (high school diploma or equivalent) and 23 hours of formal PM education. There is no work experience requirement. Our 3-day CAPM training satisfies the 23-hour requirement entirely.

fq_items.question: What is the difference between the CAPM and the PMP?
fq_items.answer: The PMP is the senior professional credential and requires 36 months of documented PM experience. The CAPM is the entry-level credential with no experience requirement. CAPM training hours count toward the PMP's 35-hour requirement, making the CAPM a structured pathway to the PMP for professionals who aren't yet experience-eligible.

fq_items.question: Is the CAPM worth it in 2026?
fq_items.answer: Yes, particularly for professionals who don't yet have the experience for the PMP. The CAPM is recognized globally by employers as evidence of formal PM knowledge. It frequently differentiates candidates for coordinator, analyst, and associate PM roles — especially those competing against candidates without any PMI credential.

fq_items.question: What is the difference between CAPM Certification Training and the CAPM Bootcamp?
fq_items.answer: Both are 3-day live-virtual courses that satisfy PMI's 23-hour requirement. The CAPM Certification Training ($795) runs at a standard pace (9am–5pm). The CAPM Bootcamp ($995) is a more intensive format (9am–6pm) designed for students who want maximum exam preparation in the shortest time. Both include our Pass Guarantee.

fq_items.question: How does the CAPM exam work?
fq_items.answer: The CAPM exam is 150 multiple-choice questions with a 3-hour time limit. It's administered by Pearson VUE at test centers or online proctored. The updated exam (2023+) includes significant agile and hybrid content alongside traditional predictive PM methodology.

fq_items.question: Do my CAPM training hours count toward the PMP?
fq_items.answer: Yes. Your 23 CAPM contact hours apply directly toward the PMP's 35-hour education requirement. When you've accumulated the required PM experience and are ready to pursue the PMP, you can document those hours plus any additional training to reach the 35-hour threshold.

fq_items.question: What is the CPMAI certification?
fq_items.answer: The CPMAI (Certified Professional in AI for Project Management) is a credential for project managers who lead or contribute to AI-driven projects. It covers the AI project lifecycle, data management for PMs, responsible AI governance, regulatory compliance, risk management, and practical AI tool application — without requiring a data science background.

fq_items.question: Do I need a technical or data science background for the CPMAI?
fq_items.answer: No. The CPMAI is designed for project managers, not data scientists. The curriculum addresses AI at the level a PM needs to lead AI projects effectively — understanding concepts, managing data-dependent work, governing AI risks, and measuring business value. No coding or ML engineering skills required.

fq_items.question: Who should get the CPMAI?
fq_items.answer: PMP-holders and experienced PMs moving into AI project delivery; technology and IT program managers overseeing digital transformation; business analysts on AI initiatives; product managers building AI-powered products; and PMO leaders who need to govern AI projects consistently across a portfolio.

fq_items.question: How long is CPMAI training?
fq_items.answer: Our CPMAI training is a 3-day program, available as live-virtual instructor-led training ($1,495) or self-paced on-demand ($795). Both formats cover the full curriculum including AI project lifecycle, responsible AI, regulatory compliance, and practical AI tool application.

fq_items.question: How many PDUs do I need to renew my PMP?
fq_items.answer: PMI requires 60 PDUs every 3 years to maintain your PMP certification. PDUs must be earned across PMI's Talent Triangle: Technical (minimum 8), Leadership (minimum 8), and Strategic (minimum 8), with the remaining 36 flexible across any category.

fq_items.question: How many PDUs do I need to renew my CAPM?
fq_items.answer: The CAPM requires 15 PDUs every 3 years, or you can retake the exam. Unlike the PMP, the CAPM renewal threshold is lower — many CAPM holders use this cycle as a natural point to transition to pursuing the PMP.

fq_items.question: What PDU courses does Granite PM Academy offer?
fq_items.answer: We offer a range of on-demand PDU courses covering leadership, agile, risk management, strategic PM, and AI for project managers. PDUs earned through our courses are eligible for reporting to PMI as education PDUs. Visit our PDU Courses page for the current catalog.

fq_items.question: How do I report PDUs to PMI?
fq_items.answer: Log in to your PMI CCRS (Continuing Certification Requirements System) account at pmi.org. Select 'Report PDUs,' choose 'Education' as the category, and enter our course details. Keep your certificate of completion as documentation — PMI may request it during audits.

fq_items.question: Are classes held in person or online?
fq_items.answer: We offer live-virtual instructor-led training via video conference — not pre-recorded video. A real PMI-certified instructor leads every session in real time. We also offer self-paced on-demand access for most courses. In-person sessions are available for corporate groups on request.

fq_items.question: What is the difference between Live-Virtual and On-Demand?
fq_items.answer: Live-virtual is a real-time online classroom with a live instructor — you attend scheduled sessions, ask questions, and interact with other students. On-demand is self-paced recorded content you access on your own schedule with no fixed class times. Both formats satisfy PMI's contact hour requirements.

fq_items.question: What are the course prices?
fq_items.answer: PMP Certification Training: $1,995 (live-virtual) / $995 (on-demand). PMP Bootcamp: $2,495 (live-virtual). CAPM Certification Training: $795 (live-virtual) / $495 (on-demand). CAPM Bootcamp: $995 (live-virtual). CPMAI Training: $1,495 (live-virtual) / $795 (on-demand). Group pricing is available for teams of 3 or more.

fq_items.question: Can my employer pay for training?
fq_items.answer: Yes — most employers cover PM certification training through professional development or L&D budgets. We provide formal invoicing, purchase orders, and group enrollment support. Veterans: GI Bill and DOD SkillBridge programs may also apply depending on your eligibility status.

fq_items.question: What if I need to reschedule my class?
fq_items.answer: We understand schedules change. Contact us at least 5 business days before your session start date to transfer to another session at no charge. Rescheduling requests within 5 business days may be subject to a transfer fee. On-demand students can access content at any time and don't have fixed session dates.

fq_items.question: Do I get a certificate of completion?
fq_items.answer: Yes. All students receive a certificate of completion documenting their contact hours. This certificate is what you submit with your PMI application to verify you've met the education requirement. Keep it — PMI may request it during audits of your application or renewal cycle.

fq_items.question: Is there group pricing for teams?
fq_items.answer: Yes. We offer group pricing for teams of 3 or more enrolling in the same course. We can also schedule dedicated private sessions for corporate groups. Contact us with your team size and desired course to get a custom quote.

fq_items.question: What technology do I need for live-virtual training?
fq_items.answer: A computer with a stable internet connection, a webcam, and a microphone. We use a standard video conferencing platform — no special software installation required beyond what you likely already use for work. We'll send connection details after enrollment.
